Text: Let's understand binary search in 60 seconds for your next interview.
Binary search is an efficient way to find a target value within a sorted list by repeatedly splitting the search area in half.
This method relies entirely on the fact that the numbers are already in order.
We start with two boundaries, a left marker at the beginning and a right marker at the end.
To find our target, we look at the exact middle of these two points.
We compare the value at this middle position to our target.
If the middle value is exactly what we are looking for, the search ends immediately.
If the middle value is greater than our target, we know the target cannot be on the right side.
Because every number there is even larger, we move our right marker to the middle, discarding the entire right half.
If the middle value is smaller than our target, we know the target cannot be on the left.
Every number to the left is even smaller.
We move our left marker to the middle, discarding the entire left half by repeating this,
calculating the middle and moving a boundary, we narrow the search space until the target is found or the marker's meet.
In an interview, remember that this takes logarithmic time, or log n,
because we divide the problem by two with every single check, problem solved.
